_comment: deterministic pool — script picks by hash(date) % len. don't reorder unless you want everyone's joke to shift.
jokes: Why do programmers prefer dark mode? Because light attracts bugs.
There are 10 kinds of people in this world: those who understand binary, and those who don't.
A SQL query walks into a bar, walks up to two tables and asks: 'Can I join you?'
I would tell you a UDP joke, but you might not get it.
Programming is 10% writing code and 90% understanding why it's not working.
How many programmers does it take to change a lightbulb? None. That's a hardware problem.
!false — it's funny because it's true.
Debugging: being the detective in a crime movie where you're also the murderer.
There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-one errors.
A programmer's wife tells him: 'Go to the store and get a loaf of bread. If they have eggs, get a dozen.' He comes home with 12 loaves of bread.
Why did the developer go broke? Because he used up all his cache.
'Knock knock.' 'Who's there?' very long pause ... 'Java.'
git push --force is the 'delete this' button of senior engineers.
I used to be a banker, but I lost interest.
In C we had to code our own bugs. In C++ we can inherit them.
A good programmer is someone who looks both ways before crossing a one-way street.
Two bytes meet. The first byte asks, 'Are you ill?' The second byte replies, 'No, just feeling a bit off.'
A programmer's diet: ctrl-s, ctrl-c, ctrl-v, repeat.
Real programmers count from 0.
My code doesn't work — I have no idea why. My code works — I have no idea why.
There is no cloud. It's just someone else's computer.
I'm not lazy, I'm on energy-saving mode.
Why do Java developers wear glasses? Because they don't C#.
What's the object-oriented way to become wealthy? Inheritance.
Roses are red, violets are blue, unexpected '{' on line 32.
My code is flawless. The input is just incorrect.
'I before E except after C.' Weird, science says otherwise.
A programmer had a problem. He decided to use Java. He now has a ProblemFactory.
Algorithm: word used by programmers when they don't want to explain what they did.
The best thing about booleans: even if you're wrong, you're only off by a bit.
Software and cathedrals are much the same — first we build them, then we pray.
If debugging is the process of removing bugs, then programming must be the process of putting them in.
A byte walks into a restaurant and asks for a bit to eat.
XML is like violence — if it doesn't solve your problem, you aren't using enough of it.
There are two kinds of people: those who back up their work, and those who have never had a hard drive fail.
Why don't programmers like nature? It has too many bugs.
Hardware: the parts of a computer that can be kicked.
There are 2 types of people in the world: those who can extrapolate from incomplete data.
Optimism in software engineering: 'this should only take an afternoon.'
When your code works on the first try: suspicion intensifies.
Recursion: see recursion.
To understand recursion, one must first understand recursion.
I don't always test my code, but when I do, I do it in production.
Commit messages like 'fixed stuff' are how civilizations fall.
Stack Overflow: the real source of truth. Docs are just marketing.
The user is not broken. Your assumptions are.
Agile: code now, plan never, blame everyone.
Scrum master: the person who asks why you're behind on a story you haven't started.
Daily standup: a ritual where we all pretend the status has changed since yesterday.
Technical debt: the interest you pay on shortcuts past-you took.
Refactor: rewriting code that already works so that it works slightly differently.
Legacy code: code that works.
TDD: test-driven development, or terror-driven debugging?
Senior engineer: knows which bugs to leave alone.
Junior engineer: fixes the bug, breaks three features.
DevOps: a cry for help dressed up as a job title.
The best error message is no error message.
Error 404: joke not found.
Ctrl+Z is a love language.
Python is the second-best language for everything. This is its greatest strength.
JavaScript: a language where NaN !== NaN and you just have to be okay with that.
PHP: it's not dead, it just smells funny.
Rust: where the compiler is meaner than your code reviewer.
Go: simplicity through the absence of features.
TypeScript: JavaScript wearing a suit to a job interview.
CSS: the only language where you spend 3 hours to center a div.
HTML is not a programming language. Neither is your MBA.
Assembly: making CPUs happy, developers sad.
Perl: write-only language.
Haskell: where monads live, and grad students cry.
SQL: because data isn't going to organize itself.
NoSQL: because schemas are for cowards.
Kubernetes: a distributed system for managing your distributed systems.
Docker: it works on my machine, now containerized.
Microservices: turning one problem into forty smaller, networked problems.
Serverless: someone else's server.
AI won't take your job. Someone using AI will.
Prompt engineering: begging a chatbot in natural language.
Machine learning: fancy curve-fitting with more GPUs.
Deep learning: when regular learning isn't pretentious enough.
Data science: statistics, but the salary's higher.
Blockchain: a spreadsheet that doesn't trust itself.
NFT: right-click-save-as, now with extra steps.
Web3: solutions looking for problems.
The metaverse: Zoom but worse.
Programming in 1970: punchcards. Programming in 2026: importing 400MB to console.log('hi').
Left-pad: lessons learned, then forgotten, then relearned.
npm install: and now you have 4 GB of node_modules.
My favorite design pattern is copying code from Stack Overflow.
The cheapest, fastest, and most reliable components are those that aren't there.
Walking on water and developing software from a specification are easy if both are frozen.
If at first you don't succeed, call it version 1.0.
Weeks of coding can save you hours of planning.
The best performance improvement is the transition from the nonworking state to the working state.
Documentation is a love letter you write to your future self.
Premature optimization is the root of all evil. Post-shipping optimization is also evil.
The bug you can't reproduce is the one that will take down prod.
On-call rotation: the time you spend hoping the pager stays quiet.
Standup? More like sitdown if you're working from home.
'It works on my machine' is now a valid deployment strategy, thanks to Docker.
The joy of being a developer: turning caffeine into bugs.
I would explain my code, but then I'd have to read it.
Code never lies. Comments sometimes do.
A week without sleep is like a typo — nobody notices but you.
Success is just a failure that persisted.
Success in tech: shipping before anyone asks for a demo.
Open source: strangers on the internet maintain half the internet for free.
Dependency hell: where all reasonable people end up eventually.
Licensing: the most boring part of the most exciting field.
When in doubt, blame the intern. (Never blame the intern.)
Ship it Friday: the sixth stage of grief.
Merge conflicts: the universe reminding you to communicate.
Rebase: history rewritten for a better tomorrow.
Cherry-pick: commit, but only the good parts.
Force push to main: in the off chance you wanted a new job.
Two programmers can write the same function in 14 different ways, all correct.
Abstraction: the art of hiding complexity where no one can find it.
'We'll come back and fix this later' — famous last words.
TODO is Latin for 'probably never.'
FIXME is Latin for 'definitely never.'
Side project: main project you're avoiding.
Main project: side project you agreed to deliver.
You can pry my vim keybindings from my cold, dead hands.
Emacs isn't a text editor. It's an operating system searching for a text editor.
VS Code: if Chrome had a baby with a text editor.
Tabs vs spaces: the oldest war still being fought.
Dark mode users live longer. Source: me, in the dark, saving my retinas.
Light mode exists, but so do war crimes.
Semicolons: divisive, like pineapple on pizza.
Pineapple on pizza: delicious, like semicolons.
A linter is a small dog yapping at your code.
A formatter is a small dog that already ate your code.
Autocomplete finished my sentence before I did and that's fine now.
The best code is the code you didn't write.
The second-best code is the code you deleted.
Deleted 500 lines of code; PR approved on sight.
Every time you add a feature, a test cries.
Every time you remove a feature, a user cries.
Users: never the problem, often the root cause.
Product managers: in meetings, about meetings, about meetings.
Designers: 'can you make the logo bigger?'
Engineers: 'can we make the scope smaller?'
Scope creep: the slow, silent death of every project.
'Just one more feature' — a haiku.
Stand-up: a time to practice speaking while looking at slack.
Retrospective: the meeting where we politely ignore what actually went wrong.
OKRs: optimistic goals, real regret.
KPIs: keep people in panic.
Sprint planning: guessing with extra steps.
Velocity: a made-up number used to make managers feel better.
Story points: vibes, quantified.
MVP: minimum viable product, or maximum viable procrastination.
Pivot: we changed our mind and you have to be happy about it.
Startup: a place where you wear many hats and fix many bugs.
Corporate: a place where you wear one hat and attend 40 meetings.
Hybrid work: best of both worlds, or worst of both commutes?
Fully remote: pajamas are valid business attire.
Back to office: five days of performative typing.
Slack: where work goes to die in notifications.
Email: archaic, formal, still somehow necessary.
Calendar: tetris, but you lose either way.
1:1s: a meeting to discuss all the other meetings.
Postmortem: we broke prod and wrote a beautiful document about it.
Blameless culture: we all know who did it, we just don't say it out loud.
Root cause analysis: blaming the right thing with receipts.
Feature flag: a light switch for your mistakes.
A/B test: flipping a coin with math.
Shadow traffic: pretending to serve users to see if anything explodes.
Canary deploy: a small sacrifice to the uptime gods.
Rollback: the bravest command in the DevOps dictionary.
Incident commander: babysitter with a pager.
Prod parity: a lie we tell in staging.
Flaky test: Schrödinger's CI.
Test coverage: the number that looks impressive on dashboards.
Code review: two engineers, one opinion, zero agreement.
LGTM: I skimmed it, the car is on fire, good luck.
Nitpick: the reviewer's love language.
Squash-merge: evidence management.
git blame: archaeology for regrettable decisions.
git log: a history of bad ideas and the people who approved them.
README: optional, like oxygen.
CONTRIBUTING.md: the door you slam on new contributors.
CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md: the document no one reads until something breaks.
LICENSE: nobody reads, everyone should.
SECURITY.md: the confession booth.
npm audit: 4 critical vulnerabilities you can do nothing about.
pip freeze: a snapshot of your regret.
requirements.txt: the ghost of environments past.
venv: a cage for your dependencies.
conda: a snake with opinions.
Jupyter notebook: half code, half stream of consciousness.
Data scientist: statistician with a MacBook.
Machine learning engineer: a software engineer who watched one lecture on PyTorch.
Full-stack developer: mediocre at many things.
Senior developer: knows which mediocrity to hide.
Architect: someone who draws boxes and arrows for a living.
CTO: the person who writes LinkedIn posts about tech trends.
VP of Engineering: the calendar's favorite meal.
Your computer doesn't love you back, but you should still treat it with respect.
Cache me if you can.
Race condition: when your code works 99% of the time and wakes you up at 3am the other 1%.
Deadlock: when two threads try to be polite at the same time.
Starvation: the algorithm equivalent of being picked last in gym class.
Thread-safe: just add a mutex and pray.
Async/await: syntax sugar for pain.
Promise: a future regret wrapped in a .then().
Callback hell: where code indentation goes to die.
RegEx: a way to turn one problem into two.
Unicode: because ASCII was too simple to cause problems.
Endianness: the flavor of your bytes.
Null: the billion-dollar mistake, still shipping.
Undefined: null's enigmatic sibling in JavaScript.
NaN is a number. That's the joke.
Infinity: the number bigger than the biggest number, but still less than your node_modules size.
Base64: encoded, not encrypted. Stop that.
HTTPS: the little green lock everyone judges you for missing.
TLS: technology layered security, and therapy-level stress.
OAuth: because storing passwords was too simple.
JWT: JSON Web Tokens, also known as 'we'll regret this.'
Cookies: the web's memory, also the thing lawyers love.
GDPR: making every website ask if it can be friends with you.
Cookie banner: the first 4 clicks on every site.
CAPTCHA: proving you're human by failing to identify bikes.
Passwords: should be long, random, unique, and unforgettable. Pick three.
Password manager: you trust one app with all of them. Brave choice.
Two-factor authentication: one factor is your phone, the other is hope.
Phishing: the oldest social engineering trick, still undefeated.
Ransomware: the CEO's surprise pop quiz.
Zero-day: the day before the CVE.
CVE: a hall of fame no developer wants to be in.
Patch Tuesday: the IT department's favorite holiday.
Backup: the thing you wish you had, after.
